1: Psalm 4 is the liturgical partner to Psalm 3: if Ps 3 is the morning psalm (David awoke safely, v5), Ps 4 closes the day in peace ('in peace I will lie down,' v8). The pairing of a morning and evening psalm reflects the rhythm of daily worship in Israel, framed by the morning and evening sacrifices at the tabernacle/temple (Exod 29:38-41). 'God of my righteousness (tsidqi)' — YHWH as the guarantor of the psalmist's covenant standing before the community assembly. The legal dimension is prominent: the psalmist faces opponents who have brought charges against his honor, and YHWH is the superior court before which the case is brought.
2: 'Sons of man (bene ish)' — ish (as opposed to adam, generic humanity) connotes men of standing or social rank; the social elite who have power to damage the psalmist's reputation. 'How long will you love what is worthless (reiq, empty/vain) and pursue lies' — reiq (vanity, emptiness) is the same root as the idol-critique throughout the prophets: idols are reiq because they are non-entities (Jer 10:15; Jon 2:8: 'Those who cling to worthless idols forfeit the grace that could be theirs'). The psalmist's opponents are pursuing the same futility as idolaters — placing confidence in that which cannot deliver.
3: 'The LORD has set apart the faithful (hasid) for himself; the LORD hears when I call to him' — hasid is the term for the one who practices hesed (covenant loyalty), not merely the recipient of divine love but the active covenant partner who reciprocates faithfulness. The word hasid will later give rise to the Hasidim — the 'pious ones' of the Maccabean period (165 BCE) who chose death over Torah violation under Antiochus Epiphanes. 'The LORD hears when I call' asserts the covenant communication network: YHWH is the accessible patron who does not require costly intermediation through human brokers.
4: 'Tremble with awe and do not sin; search your hearts in silence on your beds' — the Septuagint renders 'be angry and sin not,' the Greek phrase Paul quotes verbatim in Eph 4:26 as ethical instruction about anger management. The Hebrew more naturally means reverent trembling (cf. Hab 3:16). Night-time interior self-examination — silent, on one's bed — is a mode of piety without close parallel in ANE religion, which was publicly performative and communal. The Psalter here commends private, interior, self-reflective spirituality as authentic worship — a practice the Sermon on the Mount (Matt 6:6: 'pray in your room and shut the door') will develop further.
5: 'Offer right sacrifices (zivchei tsedek), and put your trust in the LORD' — the two inseparable dimensions of covenant worship: the exterior cultic act and the interior disposition. The 'right' sacrifice is not merely ritually correct but morally grounded (cf. Ps 51:17: 'The sacrifices of God are a broken spirit'). The covenant framework required both: ritual without trust is hollow manipulation; trust without cultic expression fails to engage the concrete covenantal structure YHWH established. The pairing resists both the error of purely external religion and the opposite error of purely interior spirituality that dismisses covenant practice.
6: 'Let the light of your face shine on us, O LORD!' — direct allusion to the Aaronic priestly blessing of Num 6:24-26: 'the LORD make his face to shine upon you and be gracious to you; the LORD lift up his countenance upon you and give you peace.' The priestly blessing was the official covenant benediction pronounced over the assembly at the conclusion of worship; invoking it here is to ask for the full weight of YHWH's covenant favor. The 'face' (panim) of YHWH signifies his personal, attentive presence — the opposite of YHWH 'hiding his face' in judgment (Ps 13:1; 27:9).
7: 'You have placed more joy in my heart than they have when their grain and wine overflow' — the grain and wine (dagan and tirosh) are the classic twin indicators of covenant blessing in Deut 7:13 (promised to the obedient as land's produce) and Hos 2:8 (which Israel wrongly attributed to Baal). The comparison is not between spiritual and material goods but between two competing sources of joy: the presence of YHWH vs. the abundance of the land's produce. That the psalmist prefers the former is a deliberate covenant priority — the Giver over the gift.
8: 'In peace I will lie down and sleep, for you alone, O LORD, make me dwell in safety' — the evening bookend to Psalm 3's morning security (Ps 3:5). Sleep was the moment of maximum vulnerability in antiquity: military attack, wild animals, fire, illness — all struck most effectively at night. The ability to sleep peacefully was the existential test of covenant confidence. Prov 3:24 uses the same image: 'If you lie down, you will not be afraid; when you lie down, your sleep will be sweet.' Safety is not achieved by human arrangement but by the exclusive ('you alone') divine guarantee.
